results.possibleIdentity: Jo Rohn was a technical professional and architect active in Toronto, Ontario, during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was a principal or key member of the Sussex Research Group (also known as Sussex Research), an interdisciplinary architectural and technical research firm that operated out of 243 Borden Street (later 207 Borden Street) in Toronto. Rohn's work was characterized by a combination of architecture, structural engineering, and computer programming. He notably collaborated with architect Harald Ensslen on the development of 'PIOGRAM,' a project or computer-based system focused on urban tenement housing on a low-rise, high-density scale, featuring interior pedestrian streets and weather-protected arcades. This work was documented in technical reports, including those archived at MIT. Rohn and the Sussex Research Group were part of a vibrant technical and artistic community in Toronto that sought to apply engineering and programming techniques to social and architectural problems. He is associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that promoted collaborations between artists and engineers, and likely participated in the activities of the E.A.T. Toronto branch during its active years in the early 1970s.
